DETAIL.HISTORICAL_DESC: Discover the deep-rooted history and architectural marvels that define {{name}}.
DETAIL.NO_PHOTOS: No photos available for this destination yet.
DETAIL.VIDEO_INFO: Watch a video tour of this beautiful location.
DETAIL.OFFER_NO_RESULTS: No offers available for this category.

DESCRIPTION.Jemaa el-Fnaa: Jemaa el-Fnaa Square has been the beating heart of Marrakech since the founding of the city in the 11th century by the Almoravids. Historically, it served as a place of public justice where criminals were beheaded, hence its macabre name which means 'assembly of the dead'. Over the centuries, this space has transformed into a crossroads of trade and cultural encounters, attracting caravanners from all over the world. Its cultural importance is such that UNESCO declared it a masterpiece of the oral and intangible heritage of humanity. Today, it perpetuates Moroccan traditions through its storytellers, its Gnaoua musicians, and its snake charmers, representing a true open-air theater which illustrates the richness and diversity of Moroccan heritage.
DESCRIPTION.Hassan II Mosque: The Hassan II Mosque, erected on the waves of the Atlantic Ocean in Casablanca, is a monumental masterpiece of contemporary Islamic architecture. Completed in 1993 at the request of the late King Hassan II, it symbolizes the rebirth of traditional Moroccan craftsmanship combined with modern technologies. Thousands of artisans from across the kingdom contributed to the creation of its intricate zelliges, carved cedar ceilings, and delicate stucco. Historically, it marks the desire to provide the economic capital with a spiritual monument of international scope. Its minaret, rising to 210 meters, was for a long time the tallest in the world. This monument is not only a place of worship, but a true emblem of Moroccan identity, testifying to the architectural genius and religious fervor of the country.
DESCRIPTION.Ait Ben Haddou: Aït-ben-Haddou is a ksar of Morocco inscribed on the UNESCO World Heritage List since 1987. Located in the province of Ouarzazate, it is a striking example of traditional South Moroccan architecture. This fortified village, made up of a group of earthen buildings surrounded by walls, was once an important stopover for caravans linking the Sahara to Marrakech. Its compact structure, its corner towers and its decorative patterns in raw brick bear witness to the architectural genius of the pre-Saharan populations. The site has also served as the setting for many famous international films, such as Lawrence of Arabia, Gladiator and Kingdom of Heaven, contributing to its worldwide fame.
DESCRIPTION.Merzouga Desert: The Merzouga desert, with its imposing Erg Chebbi dunes which can reach up to 150 meters in height, is a geological marvel of southeastern Morocco. Historically, this region was inhabited by nomadic Amazigh tribes who developed an intimate knowledge of arid ecosystems and oasis routes. Local legend has it that these gigantic dunes were shaped by God to punish a rich local family who refused to shelter a poor pilgrim. Beyond its striking natural beauty, Merzouga has long been a crossroads for trans-Saharan routes, a vital trade hub that has enriched the cultural diversity of the area. Today, the region preserves its unique Saharan heritage and offers a fascinating insight into ancient life driven by camel caravans.
DESCRIPTION.Ouzoud Waterfalls: The Ouzoud Waterfalls, nestled in the heart of the majestic Middle Atlas Mountains, are one of North Africa's most spectacular natural attractions, crashing fiercely from an impressive height of 110 meters. Having the meaning of olive in Amazigh, Ouzoud testifies to the importance of the cultivation of the olive tree which has shaped the economic life of the Berbers of the region for generations. Historically, the site was home to numerous small traditional water mills, the remains of which still dot the banks of the El Abid River, illustrating an ingenious ancestral agricultural system. These sacred places are also home to communities of Barbary macaques, endemic and respected by locals. Visiting Ouzoud means soaking up a harmonious blend of Moroccan rural history, local legends and unaltered nature, all bathed in the myths of Amazigh mythology.
DESCRIPTION.Fes El Bali: Fez el-Bali, the oldest fortified medina in Fez, was founded at the end of the 8th century by the Idrisid dynasty. With its incredible network of nearly 9,000 intertwining alleys, this urban masterpiece is recognized as the largest pedestrian zone in the world and listed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site. Historically, Fez has been the revolutionary intellectual, cultural and spiritual heart of Morocco, and is even home to the University of Al Quaraouiyine, founded in 859 by Fatima al-Fihri, recognized globally as the oldest university in the world. The richness of its Andalusian and Arab-Muslim influenced architecture, its sublime madrassahs like the Bou Inania, and its impressive mazes symbolize the golden age of the Islamic kingdom. The legacy of tanners, copper artisans and ceramicists still resonates vigorously, keeping centuries-old traditional methods alive.
DESCRIPTION.Todgha Gorges: The Todgha (or Todra) gorges are made up of monumental red limestone cliffs reaching up to 300 meters in height on the eastern flanks of the High Atlas mountains. Cut centuries by the eponymous course, the immense fault valley historically represented a difficult strategic path for trans-Saharan Arab and Berber merchants. These impressive natural walls therefore formed impregnable defensive spaces, once housing majestic kasbahs intended to protect agricultural crops as well as golden caravans. Culturally, the region is deeply influenced by the pioneering and enduring spirit of Tinghir residents. By embodying the perseverance of rural populations, Todgha magnificently exalts the ancient ingenuity demonstrated by the inhabitants in irrigating a green oasis despite the surrounding arid desert atmosphere.
DESCRIPTION.Dades Valley: The spectacular Dadès Valley, popularly nicknamed 'the valley of a thousand kasbahs', carves its way through astonishing rocky outcrops, where the mysterious round rocks of the famous 'Monkey Fingers' range dominate. Throughout generations, the Dades River has miraculously sculpted the landscape into a lush environment renowned for its alluring beauty in contrast to the surrounding desert. Rich in a dense history, the countless ksours and kasbahs served as resilient defenses for the Amazigh clans against any intrusion from the southern regions. The road that lies there once hosted a continuous traffic of traders crisscrossing formidable trans-Saharan routes. These clay architectures of unequaled finesse, vital reservoirs of Moroccan geological memory, evoke with passion and wonder a regional past of courageous conquests and survival.
DESCRIPTION.Essaouira Medina: The charming medina of Essaouira, formerly known under the illustrious name of Mogador, is a fortified coastal jewel renowned for the melody of its powerful sea winds and its vibrant cultural diversity. Built at the end of the 18th century by fine European architects such as Théodore Cornut, on the careful commission of Sultan Sidi Mohammed ben Abdallah, it was specifically designed to channel international maritime trade towards the distant riches of the interior of the country. This astonishing orthogonal layout rubs shoulders in rare harmony with its famous Skala adorned with old Spanish cannons. Essaouira breathes a deep cosmopolitan past where Jews, Christians and Muslims coexisted fraternally, a tolerant heritage cherished and carefully preserved. Rightly classified in the UNESCO register, the city continues to bear witness to a living Gnoua cultural expression in the present.
DESCRIPTION.Rabat City Center: Rabat, the dignified political and administrative capital of the Kingdom of Morocco, enjoys a captivating fusion of medieval Muslim heritage and splendid harmonious colonial town planning. Seriously built in the 12th century by the reckless Almohad dynasty under the aegis of the conquering caliph Yaqub al-Mansur, the Rabat peninsula became the strategic departure point for the formidable fleets en route to Al-Andalus. Developing with splendor a prestigious number of monumental ramparts and captivating attractions such as the majestic Hassan Tower and the aerial Marinid Necropolis of Chellah, the city was also an imposing retreat for intellectuals, noble artisans, as well as exiled buccaneers. Recognized jointly with Fez and Marrakech as world cultural heritage, the resplendent metropolis humbly and proudly displays a precious memory combining yesterday's grandeur with contemporary refined modernity.
DESCRIPTION.Agadir Beach: The splendor of the seaside city of Agadir, located along Morocco's vibrant Atlantic coast, presents an exceptionally touching urban and human resilience. After being destroyed during a cataclysmic earthquake in February 1960 which took away its authentic architecture, the municipality was completely redesigned and valiantly elevated towards visionary modernism. Under the aegis of the slogan 'Agadir will not die', dictated by the late King Mohammed V, the restructuring gave rise to a seismic planning method ultimately achieving a new seaside presence of unparalleled reliability. The only survivors of this misfortune remain the majestic silent ruins of the Ancient Kasbah of Oufella. Historically representing an important coastal shipping port on the route to Senegal under former powers, the recent version boldly advocates a famous hub of peaceful tourist freedom.
DESCRIPTION.Kelaat Mgouna: Kalâat M'Gouna, poetically built in the foothills of the Central Atlas, is internationally baptized the proud 'Valley of Roses' thanks to moving floral know-how dating from the last century. Once founded on a flourishing land of ancestral barter where hardened caravanners negotiated luxuriously, the town was sublimated by the planting of precious Damascus rose bushes, supposedly brought back during pious pilgrimages from Syria. Ritually dedicated to the exhaustive cultivation of Rosa damascena, this vast floral decoration offers a majestic aromatic production which delivers the famous soothing 'rose water' known worldwide today. The remaining vestiges of its colossal original Kasbah majestically recount a proud and fascinating Amazigh heritage, symbolizing the rich local synergy with this resplendent botanical exuberance.
DESCRIPTION.Sefrou City: The captivating Sefrou, curiously described as 'the beauty that protects', is affectionately one of the extremely rare ancient resilient urban oases at the infinite foot of the Atlantic slopes. Wonderfully nestled next to preciously fertile cherry orchards, the intelligent locality historically flourished half a millennium before the prestigious Fez. The great Moulay Idriss II had even stayed there decisively during the beginnings of imperial consolidation around the year 808. Known globally through an exceptional multiculturalism where so many brilliant philosophical rabbis resided near the sacred Mellah district, the urban crossroads was naturally an essential essential stopover on sub-Saharan routes. Now decorated with its summer festivity called 'Cherry Festival', it gloriously expresses a pleasant and truly authentic and diverse Moroccan past.
DESCRIPTION.Guelmim Gate: Guelmim, affectionately called with honor and mystery 'the gateway to the Sahara', generally constitutes the major provincial border metropolis of yesteryear towards the eternal aridity of the enormous regular Atlantic basin. Historically vital, it was an opulent replica of the famous Tafilalt road, truly building the essential and grand terminal of an epic trans-Saharan interactive passage of immense nomadic caravans. The prosperous capital designed in the heart of enclosed valleys continually and proudly hosts the most famous historic weekly camel market 'Amhouagsh' in all of Africa. Its charismatic presence preserves unaltered the rich and living Hassani culture, glorifying the authentic Bedouin values ​​of hospitable hosts who courageously perpetuated the masterful and peaceful sub-Saharan interregional control.
DESCRIPTION.Laayoune City: Boldly established in the arid extraordinary latitudes of the majestic great Sahara, Laâyoune once emerged as a strategic colonial garrison designed by the Spanish general Antonio de Oro in the 1930s. The wealthy foundation had a sedentarization plan, based curiously around a miraculously exuberant well which gave it its singular name (Laâyoune meaning 'the sources'). Its famous reintegration, with the heroic Green March of a patriotic people in November 1975, undoubtedly marked its restoration to the cradle of the kingdom of Morocco. This majestic metropolis is the worthy unifying symbol indissolubly uniting national integrity with the vast majestic nomadic tribes. Having modernized dramatically since then, the city today combines unique modern Saharan architecture with important traces of its rich past.
DESCRIPTION.Imilchil Village: The legendary and majestic humble citadel of the small, remote village of Imilchil is proudly nestled at an altitude of more than 2,119 meters in the heart of the central High Atlas. The untold mysteries of this place resonate loudly, notably through the memorable and tragic myth of the legendary loves of Tislit and Isli. Coming from enemy ancestral tribes (Aït Haddou and Aït Ibrahim), the indescribable passion prevented the young lovers from uniting, leading to the creation of colossal twin lakes (Isli and Tislit) formed by the torrent of their tears! As a result, a peacemaking ceremony was established, creating the annual 'Engagement Moussem' allowing peaceful love between tribes. It is a strong heritage, deeply anthropological, which unconditionally honors marital friendship, peace, and ancient mountainous Berber customs.
DESCRIPTION.Dakhla Lagoon: A formidable sea cape of exquisite oceanographic poetry, the splendid city of the Oued Ed-Dahab peninsula, Dakhla, was majestically built during the Hispano-Saharan era. Originally established as 'Villa Cisneros' in 1884 by the admirable Emilio Bonelli, the town is historically famous as a military location and telegraph station, and was home to the first key landing strip in the south for the illustrious Aéropostale (frequented by pioneers such as Jean Mermoz and Antoine de Saint-Exupéry). Dakhla was singularly reinvigorated after the liberation of Moroccan territorial integrity, going from a simple fishing village to a thriving metropolis and global kitesurfing hub. The curious mixture of ocher desert dunes and azure ocean waters preciously celebrates its vital place and its miraculous development in the face of harsh nature.
DESCRIPTION.Al Hoceima Bay: Lulled by the melancholy of the resplendent shores of the Mediterranean, Al Hoceima rose recklessly on the mountainous slopes of the formidable Rif mountains. This city on the north coast was once established by the Spanish military colonies under General Sanjurjo after harsh wars of rebellions and the epic Rif Republic of 1921 led by the legendary Abdelkrim El Khattabi. Its colonial name was for a time 'Villa Sanjurjo', erected in its splendid presence preciously facing the blue expanse. After the glorious recovery of national independence, Al Hoceima became a proud emerald representing a Rifian resilience, strong in the fascinating history and culture of its inhabitants who have always protected the singular Mediterranean anchorage of the Kingdom.
DESCRIPTION.Bin El Ouidane Lake: Decidedly fairy-tale in appearance, the alluring majestic Lake of Bin el Ouidane is a miraculously artificial water wonder, the result of colossal engineering feats of the mid-20th century. Intelligently created during the decade from 1949 to 1953, this immense famous dam taming the El Abid River represented a unique work of great hydroelectric genius in Africa. Its primary purpose was to develop extensive irrigation of the rich Tadla plain as well as to equip the nation with vital electrical resources. This audacious hydraulic juggernaut has shaped the local ecosystem in a unique way. The serene silhouettes of the ruby ​​rocks, in pure contrast with its emerald waters, evoke wonderful memories of modern and harmonious Moroccan rural development.
DESCRIPTION.Oualidia Lagoon: Oualidia, a picturesque peaceful lagoon of dazzling splendor, is majestically located on the Atlantic coast. The founding history of this marine setting dates back to the 17th century, fortified by illustrious sovereigns and specifically named after the Saadian Sultan El Oualid. In the past, the elegant natural cove was also sheltered by ancient kasbahs whose ruins still remain, intended for protection against the fearsome maritime piracy and Portuguese desires. Having become over time the undisputed capital of Moroccan national oyster farming and a sought-after paradise refuge (appreciated by the late King Mohammed V), the city exudes a perfect calm which masterfully links thousand-year-old naval history and the exquisite contemporary coastal splendor of the Moroccan Atlantic.
DESCRIPTION.Azrou Cedar Forest: Imposing and solemn, the exuberant forest of golden cedars is established in the heart of the Middle Atlas around Azrou. This legendary expanse of multi-millennial conifers remains an undeniable witness to the distant plant past, offering inestimable freshness. Azrou, meaning 'rock' in Amazigh (referring to the local volcanic peak), has always been a central mountainous urban center. Historically, this region was a strategic and rebellious theater of resistance by Berber Sanhaja tribes, which prompted the creation of educational institutions to stem dissidence, paradoxically consolidating the Moroccan elite. The famous forest is home to the legendary Gouraud Cedar, almost 800 years old, a powerful natural symbol, and is also the precious and ultimate habitat of the endemic Barbary macaque of the gentle Riffian mountains.
DESCRIPTION.Hercules Caves: The enigmatic Caves of Hercules, a stone's throw from Tangier, envelop this northern cape overlooking the perilous straits with spectacular legends. Their famous mythology derives precisely from the fictionalized creation by the demigod Hercules of the Strait of Gibraltar by separating the European and African continents with his bare hands, during one of his 12 labors. These partially natural and heavily exploited caves have two major entrances, including the famous oceanic opening admirably reproduces the inverted map of Africa, a fascinating enigma. Since Phoenician antiquity, this space was home to daring extractions of millstones by the ancient Berber inhabitants, later becoming an uncontested refuge for the underworld and exiled kings. This is the essence of centuries-old Tangier mysticism.
DESCRIPTION.Oukaimeden Resort: Nestled magnificently in the dizzying high peaks of the imposing High Atlas Mountains, at almost 2,600 meters above sea level, the sublime Oukaïmeden reigns as both the unique and highest ski resort on the African continent. Contrary to widespread beliefs, this mountain reveals an invaluable historical treasure of world renown through the richness and extraordinary conservation of thousands of prehistoric rock engravings from the Bronze Age. These mystical outdoor creations skillfully reveal the indisputable splendor of the ancient North African transhumant shepherds. Formally developed for the mountain elite during the last century and majestically transformed into a true national pride of winter pleasures, the resort serenely unifies ancient pastoral cultural sustainability with the daring icy follies of modern and daring Moroccan high-altitude tourism.
DESCRIPTION.Saidia Beach: Brilliantly nicknamed the majestic 'Blue Pearl' of the proud Eastern Mediterranean, the sublime resort of Saïdia sumptuously combines its long beach of fine golden sand and a dazzling cerulean ocean. On the distant outskirts of the Algerian-Moroccan borders, this serene paradise originally sheltered, at the end of the 19th century, a modest but decisive fortress (kasbah) commissioned by the intrepid Sultan Hassan I with the sole aim of pacifying border unrest and sealing sacred royal integrity. Growing from a discreet isolated sentinel to the establishment of a serene family resort around the 1910s, then totally propelled recently by massive royal ambitions, the city luxuriously displays a perfect seaside cheerfulness resilient to the pure pampered summer happiness of the radiant Oriental region.
DESCRIPTION.Asilah Old Town: The dazzling Asilah, a graceful, bright and immaculate medina standing boldly against the Atlantic waves, seduces pilgrims with its unwavering poetic tranquility. Its proud, impressive ramparts erected invincibly in the 15th century chronicle the captivating and turbulent era of Portuguese colonial rule, serving fiercely as an invincible outpost before being gloriously liberated under the rule of Al Mansour Eddahbi around 1589. A famously daring multicultural pirate lair in the twilight of yesteryear, this bastion of unalterable white dazzling is today universally sacred as the prodigious Moroccan center of splendid urban visual arts. Through its famous majestic walls covered with colorful frescoes, its imposing historical bastions and its calm alleys, Asilah exalts a captivating artistic aura celebrating a magnificently restored maritime heritage of Mozarabic influences.

HISTORIQUE.Festival Gnaoua: The Essaouira Gnaoua and World Music Festival is a musical event that blends traditional Gnaoua music with contemporary musical styles from around the world, creating a unique cultural experience in the historic city of Essaouira.
HISTORIQUE.Mawazine: Mawazine — Rhythms of the World was founded in 2001 by the Maroc Cultures Foundation as a festival aimed at opening Morocco to the global music scene. From its modest beginnings, it has become one of the world's largest music festivals in terms of attendance, welcoming several million spectators each year across multiple free stages in Rabat. This festival symbolizes Morocco's cultural openness and its ambition to position itself as a crossroads of cultures between Africa, the Arab world, and the West. Over the years, Mawazine has hosted legendary artists such as Rihanna, Stevie Wonder, and Elton John, making the Moroccan capital a top-tier international music destination.
HISTORIQUE.Timitar: The Timitar festival in Agadir celebrates Amazigh culture through music and art, and serves as a bridge of communication between Amazigh artists and their counterparts from different parts of the world.
HISTORIQUE.Fes Sacred Music: The Fes Festival of World Sacred Music combines spirituality and art, where musicians from different religions and cultures meet to promote the values of peace and tolerance.
HISTORIQUE.Rose Festival: The Rose Festival in Kelaat M'Gouna is an annual celebration of the rose harvest season, highlighting the region's cultural heritage and promoting local rose-derived products.
HISTORIQUE.Tanjazz: The Tanjazz festival is an annual rendezvous for jazz lovers, bringing together great international artists in the charming spaces of the city of Tangier, bringing an atmosphere of sophistication and creativity.
HISTORIQUE.Film Festival: The Marrakech International Film Festival is one of the world's most prominent film festivals, attracting movie stars and filmmakers from around the globe to honor the seventh art.
HISTORIQUE.Cherry Festival: The Cherry Festival in Sefrou is the oldest festival in Morocco, celebrating the cherry harvest season and characterized by the election of Miss Cherry in a traditional festive atmosphere.
HISTORIQUE.Camel Festival: The Camel Moussem in Guelmim is a cultural and economic manifestation that highlights the Saharan heritage and the bedouin's relationship with the camel, with the organization of races and folk performances.
HISTORIQUE.Sahara Marathon: The Sahara Marathon in Dakhla is a global sporting challenge that brings together runners of different nationalities to compete in the heart of the Moroccan Sahara sands and within its picturesque nature.
HISTORIQUE.Imilchil Marriage: The Imilchil Moussem is a unique social and cultural event that embodies the traditions of the High Atlas tribes in choosing life partners, and attracts visitors to discover the region's authenticity.
HISTORIQUE.Tan-Tan Moussem: The Tan-Tan Moussem, classified by UNESCO as intangible world heritage, is a major cultural event that brings together desert tribes to celebrate their ancestral traditions and customs.
HISTORIQUE.Kitesurf World Cup: The Dakhla Kitesurf World Cup is a major global stop that attracts the best competitors in this water sport, taking advantage of Dakhla's exceptional natural conditions.
HISTORIQUE.Cedar Festival: The Ifrane Cedar Festival celebrates the Middle Atlas cedar forest, one of the most precious forest ecosystems in North Africa, a refuge for the iconic Barbary macaque. Ifrane, nicknamed the 'Switzerland of Morocco' for its snowy alpine landscapes, is the natural guardian of these thousand-year-old forests, some of whose trees, like the legendary Gouraud Cedar, are over 800 years old. The festival raises awareness among visitors and young Moroccans about the conservation of this ecosystem threatened by global warming and human activities, while celebrating the unique beauty of the Middle Atlas through guided hikes, scientific exhibitions, and Amazigh cultural events.
HISTORIQUE.FICAM Meknes: The Meknes International Animation Film Festival (FICAM) is the only African festival entirely dedicated to animation cinema and has become an unmissable event for professionals and amateurs of the 7th animated art. Created in the imperial city of Meknes, one of the four historic capitals of Morocco, FICAM brings together studios, directors, and students from all over the world every year for screenings, masterclasses, and creative workshops. Its vocation is to stimulate the emergence of a Moroccan and African animation industry, encouraging young talents to tell their stories with contemporary digital tools while drawing on the richness of the visual and narrative traditions of the Arab and Berber worlds.

help_center.title: Help Center
help_center.welcome_title: Welcome to the Safar Morocco Help Center
help_center.welcome_desc: Everything you need to know about using our platform.
help_center.navigate_desc: Our platform is designed to be intuitive. Use the navigation bar to explore destinations, find upcoming events, or view your personalized itineraries on the interactive map.
help_center.itineraries_desc: Go to the "Itineraries" section to start planning your trip. You can add destinations, set dates, and organize your daily activities easily.
help_center.events_desc: Discover local festivals, workshops, and tours in the "Events" section. Simply select an event and follow the booking process to secure your spot.
help_center.reservations_desc: Access your profile to view and manage all your active reservations, past trips, and saved destinations.

terms_of_service.title: Terms of Service
terms_of_service.acceptance_desc: By accessing and using Safar Morocco, you agree to be bound by these Terms of Service and all applicable laws and regulations.
terms_of_service.license_desc: Permission is granted to temporarily download one copy of the materials on Safar Morocco's website for personal, non-commercial transitory viewing only.
terms_of_service.usage_desc: Users are responsible for maintaining the confidentiality of their accounts and for all activities that occur under their account.
terms_of_service.content_desc: Users may post reviews and photos. By doing so, you grant Safar Morocco a non-exclusive, royalty-free license to use, reproduce, and display such content.
terms_of_service.modifications_desc: Safar Morocco may revise these terms of service for its website at any time without notice.

privacy_policy.title: Privacy Policy
privacy_policy.collection_desc: We collect information you provide directly to us, such as when you create an account, make a reservation, or contact us for support.
privacy_policy.use_desc: We use the information we collect to provide, maintain, and improve our services, including processing your reservations and sending you travel updates.
privacy_policy.handling_desc: Your reservation details and personal information are handled with strict confidentiality and are only shared with relevant partners (like event organizers) to facilitate your bookings.
privacy_policy.reviews_desc: Public reviews and ratings you provide will be visible to other users of the platform to help them make informed travel decisions.
privacy_policy.security_desc: We implement a variety of security measures to maintain the safety of your personal information when you enter, submit, or access your personal information.

INTRO.TITLE: 1. Introduction
INTRO.DESC: Welcome to Safar Morocco, your premier platform for discovering and booking unique travel experiences across the Kingdom. By accessing or using our website, services, or mobile applications, you agree to be bound by these Terms and Conditions. If you do not agree with any part of these terms, please refrain from using our platform.

ACCOUNTS.TITLE: 2. User Accounts
ACCOUNTS.DESC: To access certain features of Safar Morocco, you must create an account. You agree to:

LIST.1: Provide accurate, current, and complete information during the registration process.
LIST.2: Maintain and promptly update your account information.
LIST.3: Protect your login credentials and maintain the security of your password.
LIST.4: Accept all risks of unauthorized access to your account based on your failure to protect your credentials.

RESERVATIONS.TITLE: 3. Reservations and Offers
RESERVATIONS.DESC: Safar Morocco facilitates bookings for various tourism activities and accommodations. Please note that:

LIST.1: All reservations made through our platform are subject to availability and confirmation by the service provider.
LIST.2: Prices and availability are subject to change without notice until a booking is confirmed.
LIST.3: The platform acts as an intermediary; the contract for service is directly between you and the service provider.

ACCEPTABLE_USE.TITLE: 4. Acceptable Use
ACCEPTABLE_USE.DESC: You agree not to misuse the platform. Prohibited activities include, but are not limited to:

LIST.1: Attempting to gain unauthorized access to our systems or other users' accounts.
LIST.2: Publishing harmful, offensive, or illegal content.
LIST.3: Using the platform for any fraudulent or malicious purposes.
LIST.4: Interfering with the proper working of the platform or its security features.

PRIVACY.TITLE: 5. Privacy and Data Protection
PRIVACY.DESC: At Safar Morocco, we value your privacy. We collect user information such as name, email, and activity data primarily to:

LIST.2: Personalize your experience on the platform.
LIST.3: Communicate with you regarding your bookings and offers.

PRIVACY.MORE_DETAILS: For more details, please refer to our Privacy Policy.

REVIEWS.TITLE: 6. Reviews and User Content
REVIEWS.DESC: Users are encouraged to share their experiences through reviews and ratings. However:

LIST.1: You represent that you own or have the necessary rights to the content you post.
LIST.2: Safar Morocco reserves the right to remove any content deemed inappropriate, offensive, or in violation of these terms.
LIST.3: By posting content, you grant us a non-exclusive license to use it for promotional purposes.

CHANGES.TITLE: 7. Changes to Terms
CHANGES.DESC: Safar Morocco reserves the right to update or modify these terms at any time. We will notify users of significant changes, and your continued use of the platform after such changes constitutes acceptance of the new terms.

CONTACT.TITLE: 8. Contact
CONTACT.DESC: If you have any questions or concerns regarding these Terms and Conditions, please contact us at:
